content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
 
 
May 3, 2016 
 
 
Ms. Evann Whitelam, Esq. 
Nielsen Merksame 
Parrinello Gross & Leoni LLP 
2350 Kerner Boulevard. Suite 250 
San Rafael. California 94901 
 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
 
Our File No.  16-071 
 
Dear Ms. Whitelam: 
 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of your client, Pacific Gas and 
Electric Company (PG&E) regarding the gift of travel provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
“Act”).1 Please note that this letter is based only on the facts presented, and the FPPC is not a finder 
of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71). Any advice the Commission 
provides therefore assumes that the facts you provided to us are complete and accurate. (In re 
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71). Likewise, we offer no opinion on the application of laws other 
than the Act. 
 
QUESTION 
 
 
Is shuttle transportation provided to state and local officials by PG&E in connection with a 
tour of its Diablo Canyon Power Plant a reportable gift to state and local officials? 
 
CONCLUSION 
 
No. The shuttle ride would be ‘informational material” because the tour serves primarily to 
convey information and is not a reportable gift. However, any other benefits, such as food or drinks 
provided in connection with the tour, are considered reportable gifts to the extent that they equal 
$50 or more. 
 
FACTS 
 
 
Your client, Pacific Gas & Electric Company (PG&E), plans to hold on-site tours of its 
Energy Education Center and Diablo Canyon Power Plant—both located in the San Luis Obispo 
                                                          
 
1  The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
 


File No.  A-16-071 
Page No. 2 
 
 
area—for various state and local public officials. The purpose of the tour is to provide the officials 
with a full understanding of the power plant and its role in the region and state. 
 
 
The officials will arrange for their own transportation to PG&E’s Energy Education Center, 
located 4.4 miles from the Diablo Canyon Power Plant. Once there, they will receive a guided tour 
of the Education Center covering a range of topics, from PG&E’s green vehicle fleet to the safe use 
and management of nuclear fuel. After the tour, the officials will board a private shuttle, which will 
take them on a tour of PG&E’s Diablo Canyon Power Plant. During the shuttle ride, officials will 
be able to see the plant firsthand and learn about its surroundings. The road from the Energy 
Education Center to the plant is legally inaccessible to the public, and the plant itself is closed to the 
public for security and national security reasons.  
 
You have asked whether this shuttle ride would constitute a gift to the officials under the 
Act, or if it is exempt from the Act’s gift provisions under the “informational material” exception. 
(Regulation 18942). 
 
 
ANALYSIS 
Gifts, Generally 
 
 
One purpose of the Act is to reduce improper influences on public officials through gift 
giving. Section 82028(a) of the Act defines a gift as “any payment that confers a personal benefit on 
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received,” including “a 
rebate or discount […] unless the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to 
members of the public without regard to official status.”  
 
The Act imposes three requirements on state and local officials who receive gifts: 
 
 First, state and local officials may not accept gifts totaling more than $460 in a calendar year 
from a single source. (Section 89503). 
 
 Second, certain state and local officials must report any gift of $50 or more (or gifts that 
aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the calendar year on their 
statements of economic interests. (Sections 87200 - 87210.) 
 
 Third, public official may not make, participate in making, or use their position to influence 
the outcome of a governmental decision if the decision: i) involves someone who gave the 
official a gift(s) worth $460 or more; and ii) the gift was given within 12 months leading up 
to the governmental decision. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e)). 
 
In this case, PG&E wants to provide a complimentary shuttle ride to state and local officials as 
part of a tour of its Diablo Canyon Power Plant. The issue is therefore whether the shuttle ride is a 
gift under the Act. 
 
Gifts of Travel, “Informational Material” Exception 
 
Under the Act, travel costs paid for by a third party are generally reportable gifts, since travel is 
presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public official. (Section 82028.). However, under an 


File No.  A-16-071 
Page No. 3 
 
 
exception to the Act’s gift provisions, certain “informational material” is not considered a gift. 
(Regulation 18942(a)(1)). The regulations define “informational material” as “any goods or service 
that serve primarily to convey information and that is provided to the official for the purpose of 
assisting him or her in the performance of his or her official duties…” (Regulation 18942.1). 
“Transportation provided to or in connection with” “On-site demonstrations, tours, or inspections” 
is also excepted from the gift rules as “informational material” if three conditions apply: 
 
(1) The transportation serves as the means by which the information is conveyed and is integral 
to the conveyance of the information, such as an aerial tour over an area. 
 
(2) The transportation is provided solely at the site of the demonstration, tour, or inspection, 
including to and from an area of that site that is legally inaccessible to the public. 
 
(3) The transportation is to or from a site when there is no reasonable, publicly-available 
commercial transportation available to that site, and the transportation provided is limited to 
the segment for which public transportation is not available. 
 
(Regulation 18942.1(c)). 
 
We have previously advised that the cost of bus tours that are “formally structured to convey 
information to […] officials” are “informational material” for purposes of 18942, and thus not 
subject to the Act’s gift rules. (See, e.g., Barker Advice Letter, No I-93-186) (aerial and bus tour of 
the Delta for Legislators are “informational material”). 
 
The basis for the “informational material” exception to the gift rules is well-established, and 
dates back to the Spellman opinion, in which the Commission considered facts almost identical to 
your client’s. (In re Spellman (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 16). In Spellman, the Commission found that “a 
tour of PG&E’s nuclear power plant […] is not a gift within the meaning of Section 82028,” and 
was instead “informational material,” since “the tour that is provided by PG&E, although intangible 
in nature, supplies information in a useful form and does not lend itself to any accompanying 
gratuities or special favors.” (Id. at 17.). As in your case, the official in Spellman arranged for his 
own transportation to San Luis Obispo County (paid for in that case by the state). Likewise, while 
the Act seeks to limit “financial pressures and inducements,” the purpose of PG&E’s power plant 
tour is ostensibly to facilitate the “free flow of information to officials,” which the Act is not 
intended to interfere with. (Id. at 18). 
 
Now, like in the Spellman opinion and the Barker letter, PG&E intends to provide state and 
local officials with transportation—a free shuttle ride—in connection with an on-site, educational 
tour of its power plant. You have also informed us that the purpose of the transportation is to assist 
the officials in the performance of their official duties—namely, educating them about the power 
plant and its impact on the region and state. Likewise, the proposed shuttle ride appears to meet the 
three criteria under Regulation 18942.1(c):  
 
 The shuttle ride would serve as the means by which information about the power plant is 
conveyed to the officials;  
 


File No.  A-16-071 
Page No. 4 
 
 
 The shuttle ride is provided solely at the site of the power plant tour (officials must 
arrange for their own transportation to the Energy Education Center), and the plant itself 
is in an area that is legally inaccessible to the public for safety and national security 
reasons; and  
 
 There is no reasonable, publicly-available commercial transportation available to the 
power plant site, and the shuttle ride will be limited to the power plant segment of the 
tour. 
 
Thus, PG&E’s proposed shuttle bus tour of its Diablo Canyon Power Plant for state and local 
officials falls under the “informational material” exception, and would not be subject to the Act’s 
gift rules. However, please note that any other benefits PG&E provides to the officials, such as food 
or drinks, are considered reportable gifts to the extent that they equal $50 or more. (Section 87207.)   
  
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Sincerely,  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
/s/ 
 
By: 
Toren A. Lewis 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Counsel, Legal Division 
 
TAL:jgl

$50 or more.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is shuttle transportation provided to state and local officials by PG&E in connection with a 
tour of its Diablo Canyon Power Plant a reportable gift to state and local officials?

CONCLUSION: No. The shuttle ride would be ‘informational material” because the tour serves primarily to 
convey information and is not a reportable gift. However, any other benefits, such as food or drinks 
provided in connection with the tour, are considered reportable gifts to the extent that they equal 
$50 or more.
